flashcards.front: What defines a partial order relation?
flashcards.back: A relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. Example: ⊆ on power sets.
Simplified representation of poset that omits reflexive/transitive edges, showing immediate predecessors.
A poset where every pair of elements has both LUB (join) and GLB (meet).
Contains universal bounds: 0 (bottom) and 1 (top) elements that are ≤ and ≥ all elements respectively.

flashcards.front: Name two real-world applications of lattices.
flashcards.back: 1. Type hierarchies in OOP 2. Access control in security lattices (Bell-LaPadula)
Every subset (not just pairs) has LUB and GLB. Example: Power set lattice.
Set of elements where no two are comparable under the partial order.
Element y covers x if x < y and no z exists with x < z < y. Basis for Hasse diagrams.

flashcards.front: State the absorption law in lattices.
flashcards.back: a ∨ (a ∧ b) = a and a ∧ (a ∨ b) = a for all a,b in lattice.
Lattice where meet distributes over join (and vice versa). Not all lattices are distributive.
Lattice satisfying: if a ≤ b, then a ∨ (c ∧ b) = (a ∨ c) ∧ b. Weaker than distributive.
Element that cannot be expressed as join of two other elements. Atoms in Boolean algebras.
